segments.title: The Danton class reveals France's dreadnought delay
segments.summary: France's 1908 Danton-class order is contrasted with the faster all-big-gun ships already entering British and American programs.
segments.body: The Danton class retained a mixed battery of four 12-inch and twelve 9.4-inch guns while Britain had already commissioned Dreadnought and ordered further dreadnoughts and battlecruisers. Dr. Clarke argues that their 19.25-knot speed and limited heavy-gun broadside left these 1908 ships behind a naval competition that had already changed.

evidence.note: The transcript dates the Danton order, details its mixed battery and speed, and compares it with contemporary British and American dreadnought development.

segments.title: Helgoland repeats Nassau's costly turret geometry
segments.summary: The Helgoland class improves speed and balance but retains a hexagonal battery that pays for twelve guns while bringing only eight onto a broadside.
segments.body: Germany's second dreadnought group kept Nassau's hexagonal turret layout and triple-expansion machinery, although it reached about 20.5 knots and produced balanced, attractive ships. Dr. Clarke sees the class as evidence of reactive design: Germany knew it could not match British shipyard capacity, yet repeated an expensive arrangement instead of seeking a more ambitious asymmetric answer.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Helgoland battery, engines, speed, and Germany's reaction to British construction capacity, then notes Ostfriesland's later use in air-power trials.

segments.title: Blucher's guns keep her outside the battlecruiser category
segments.summary: SMS Blucher's 8.3-inch armament marks her as a large armoured cruiser rather than a battlecruiser built around battleship-calibre guns.
segments.body: The classification turns on armament rather than size or speed alone. Dr. Clarke defines a battlecruiser as a cruiser carrying guns comparable to battleships already in service; Blucher's 8.3-inch weapons fail that test and help explain why she was poorly matched to the battlecruisers she later faced.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Blucher as a large armoured cruiser and explains the battleship-calibre-gun criterion used to distinguish battlecruisers.

segments.title: Von der Tann trades usable gun arcs for offset turrets
segments.summary: Von der Tann's eight 11-inch guns and strong belt are offset by wing turrets whose cross-deck and fore-and-aft fire risk blast damage.
segments.body: Von der Tann answered the Invincible class with eight 11-inch guns and a belt reaching 9.8 inches. The offset wing turrets could theoretically put all eight guns onto a broadside, but doing so required cross-deck fire, while shooting along the ship threatened nearby superstructure. The result was a well-protected battlecruiser whose paper firing arcs were less useful in practice.

evidence.note: The transcript gives Von der Tann's displacement, guns, turret arrangement, blast hazards, and belt protection.

segments.title: Moltke and Goeben expose two battlecruiser doctrines
segments.summary: The Moltke class leads into a contrast between Germany's battle-fleet fast wing and Britain's global trade-protection and rapid-reaction battlecruisers.
segments.body: Moltke and Goeben retained 11-inch guns and offset turrets while reaching high speeds, with Goeben later serving the Ottoman Empire. Dr. Clarke contrasts Germany's plan to use battlecruisers against British scouts and around the battle line with the Royal Navy's wider mission of protecting trade, hunting cruisers, escorting troop convoys, and responding across the world's sea lanes. He argues that 11-inch guns left the German ships at a disadvantage when those missions brought them within range of British 12-inch weapons.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Moltke and Goeben, then contrasts German and British battlecruiser roles, armour, and 11-inch versus 12-inch firepower.

segments.title: Small engineering gains turn horsepower into more speed
segments.summary: The Moltke-class machinery illustrates how turbines, hull form, gearing, and propeller design could make similar power figures produce different speeds.
segments.body: Four Parsons turbines developed roughly 51,000 shaft horsepower for the Moltke class. The comparison warns against treating horsepower as a complete performance measure: hull resistance, gearing efficiency, and propeller design determine how effectively that power becomes speed. Individually modest improvements could accumulate into a meaningful advantage over successive ship classes.

evidence.note: The transcript connects Moltke-class turbine power with cumulative advances in hull design, gearing, and propeller efficiency.

segments.title: Foreign dreadnought orders preserve surge capacity
segments.summary: British-built Brazilian dreadnoughts show how export orders kept shipyards active and preserved capacity for a later emergency program.
segments.body: The 1908 fleet totals distinguish ships a navy ordered for itself from the larger capacity of its national shipbuilding industry. Britain could accept Brazilian orders when it did not need every yard for an immediate threat, sustaining skills and facilities above the level supported by domestic orders alone. American attempts to win South American, Greek, and Ottoman contracts reflected the same link between export sales and future naval mobilization.

evidence.note: The transcript reviews 1908 dreadnought totals and explains how Brazilian and other foreign orders supported British and American industrial capacity.

segments.title: British trials and German tempo create different design cycles
segments.summary: Britain fed Dreadnought's early trials into the Bellerophon class, while Germany ordered Helgoland before the Nassau class had entered service.
segments.body: Britain laid down Bellerophon as Dreadnought completed commissioning and followed with Superb and Temeraire after trials had begun exposing practical lessons. Germany, by contrast, ordered the Helgoland class before any Nassau had commissioned, committing to a second iteration without operating experience from the first. Dr. Clarke presents that timing as a central weakness in a construction program driven by reaction and status competition.

evidence.note: The transcript compares British Dreadnought and Bellerophon-class trial timing with German Nassau and Helgoland ordering and commissioning dates.
